reviewNotes: The cataloged intellectual unit is Furnivall's manuscript-specific composite representation, not the abstract Canterbury Tales tradition, the complete Gg.4.27(1) anthology, the printed carrier, or either digital encoding.

Gg.4.27(1) is the base witness, not the sole textual source. The relation locators audit every named source switch, supplied bracket, and comparison-only note in the CME XML; fragmented supplements are not flattened into continuous witness runs.

Gg.4.27(2) is a separately bound fourteen-leaf quire that does not contain this Canterbury Tales text. Its complete 40-canvas CUDL object is cached as evidence for the present object boundary, with target_work_presence set to absent; it is not a missing portion of part (1).

The Gg.4.27(1) CUDL cache retains all 1,144 provider canvases; the target Canterbury Tales is at fols. 132r–443v, canvases 275–902. The Dd.4.24 cache retains all 377 provider canvases, with relevant supplied passages at ff. 105v–106r (canvases 178–179) and f. 147r (canvas 257). The Huntington cache retains all 480 provider canvases; twenty-three exact portrait canvases support Furnivall's visual appendix.

Gamelyn is preserved as a spurious, non-Chaucerian appendix and does not increase the conventional count of twenty-four Chaucerian tales. The woodcut suites are visual apparatus, not textual witnesses to additional tales.
Royal 19 C XI supplies the French quotation printed before Melibee paragraph 2625; it is a supporting French source passage, not an English Canterbury Tales base witness.

Public access, local download status, underlying public-domain text, licenses for modern images or transcriptions, and provider publication terms are recorded separately. Cached files remain Git-ignored and are not thereby licensed for repository redistribution.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is the pinned CME XML copy of University of Michigan item AGZ8234.0001.001, a Level 1, uncorrected-OCR encoding derived from an item-specific 738-page image sequence of Frederick J. Furnivall's 1868–1879 Chaucer Society edition. Furnivall transcribes Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27(1) as his Canterbury Tales base but marks exact supplied passages from Harley 1758, Sloane 1685, Dd.4.24, Harley 7335, Royal 18 C II, Royal 17 D XV, Hatton donat. 1, and an unresolved witness identified only as Z, E, or I; quotes a French source passage from Royal 19 C XI; retains Thomas Tyrwhitt's prose paragraphing; and appends Gamelyn plus woodcuts based on Ellesmere and Gg. Complete provider objects for Gg.4.27(1), Dd.4.24, Gg.4.27(2), and Ellesmere, a complete scan of Furnivall, Internet Archive support files, the complete Dd.4.24 transcription bundle, and Digital Bodleian's complete seven-canvas Hatton selection are cached in Git-ignored source-cache files with exact URLs, checksums, and target-work locators or an explicit target-absent statement; no stable U-M bulk page-image download was verified.

catalogSummary.date: Canterbury Tales composed probably from the late 1380s through 1400; principal witness dated 1410–1440; edition published 1868–1879, with an uncertain parts-issue range through 1884
catalogSummary.region: England; Chaucer and the literary language are associated with London, while the principal Gg.4.27(1) witness was produced in East Anglia, probably near Cambridge
catalogSummary.title: The Cambridge MS (University Library, Gg. 4.27) of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales

catalogingSubject.label: The Cambridge MS (University Library, Gg. 4.27) of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The manuscript-specific textual and editorial unit represented by Frederick J. Furnivall's Chaucer Society one-text edition and the U-M item agz8234: Chaucer's unfinished Canterbury Tales in Furnivall's Gg/Furnivall sequence, with lacunae and linking passages supplied from named manuscripts, the prose Retraction, and a separately identified spurious Gamelyn appendix. The twenty-four conventional Chaucerian tales are the numbered intellectual extent; Gamelyn, a rejected Clerk link, other appended links, and the woodcut illustration suites are apparatus and do not increase that count. The unit is not the abstract Canterbury Tales tradition, any one homogeneous manuscript transcription, the complete physical Gg.4.27(1) anthology, the printed carrier, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML artifact.

contentParts.label: Group A, Fragment I
contentParts.summary: General Prologue and the Knight, Miller, Reeve, and Cook sequence; Furnivall fills General Prologue gaps from Harley 1758 and Sloane 1685 and prints the otherwise lost Cook's Tale from Sloane 1685.

contentParts.label: General Prologue
contentParts.summary: Pilgrimage-frame prologue; Gg begins within the text, and Furnivall marks supplied gaps from Harley 1758 and Sloane 1685.

contentParts.label: The Knight's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse tale; the Gg witness is extant at ff. 145r–174v, with losses documented by the catalog and edition.

contentParts.label: The Miller's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse fabliau, preceded by its prologue; the Gg tale occupies ff. 176r–184v.

contentParts.label: The Reeve's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse fabliau, preceded by its prologue; the Gg tale occupies ff. 186v–192r.

contentParts.label: The Cook's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse fragment; Gg loses the tale after its prologue, so Furnivall supplies the printed text from Sloane MS 1685.

contentParts.label: Group B-alpha, Fragment II
contentParts.summary: Man of Law sequence in Furnivall's edition order.

contentParts.label: The Man of Law's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse tale with introductory and prologue material; the Gg tale occupies ff. 196r–210v.

contentParts.label: Group D, Fragment V
contentParts.summary: Wife of Bath, Friar, and Summoner sequence; Furnivall supplies the Wife–Friar and Friar–Summoner links from Harley MS 1758.

contentParts.label: The Wife of Bath's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse romance preceded by the substantial Wife of Bath's Prologue; the Gg tale occupies ff. 222v–227v.

contentParts.label: The Friar's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse tale; the Gg witness preserves the tale at ff. 229r–232v but lacks its prologue.

contentParts.label: The Summoner's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse tale preceded by its prologue; the Gg text occupies ff. 235r–243v.

contentParts.label: Group E, Fragment VI
contentParts.summary: Clerk and Merchant sequence, including an appended rejected Clerk end-link and supplied Merchant material from Sloane 1685, Dd.4.24, and Harley 7335.

contentParts.label: The Clerk's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse tale with prologue and envoy; the Gg text occupies ff. 244r–259v, while Furnivall supplies the head-link and proem from Sloane 1685.

contentParts.label: The Merchant's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse tale; Gg preserves the tale at ff. 261r–275v but lacks its prologue. Furnivall supplies the Merchant's Prologue from Dd.4.24 and the end-link from Harley 7335.

contentParts.label: Group F, Fragment VII
contentParts.summary: Squire and Franklin sequence; Furnivall supplies links and Franklin's proem from Harley 7335 and Sloane 1685.

contentParts.label: The Squire's Tale
contentParts.summary: Unfinished verse romance; the Gg text occupies ff. 277r–284v.

contentParts.label: The Franklin's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse romance/breton lay; the Gg text occupies ff. 287r–297v.

contentParts.label: Group C, Fragment IV
contentParts.summary: Physician and Pardoner sequence; Furnivall supplies the link and first portion of the Pardoner's preamble from Sloane 1685.

contentParts.label: The Physician's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse tale; Gg preserves only a short surviving portion at ff. 302r–302v because surrounding illustrated leaves were removed.

contentParts.label: The Pardoner's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse exemplum with prologue; the Gg tale occupies ff. 306r–312v.

contentParts.label: Group B-beta, Fragment III
contentParts.summary: Shipman, Prioress, Sir Thopas, Melibee, Monk, and Nun's Priest sequence, with several links supplied from Sloane 1685, Royal 18 C II, and Royal 17 D XV.

contentParts.label: The Shipman's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse fabliau; the Gg text occupies ff. 314r–317v.

contentParts.label: The Prioress's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse Marian miracle tale; the Gg text occupies ff. 321r–322v.

contentParts.label: The Tale of Sir Thopas
contentParts.summary: Unfinished verse parody of romance; the Gg text occupies ff. 323r–324v.

contentParts.label: The Tale of Melibee
contentParts.summary: Prose moral tale; the Gg text occupies ff. 327r–349v, and Furnivall retains Tyrwhitt's paragraph divisions.

contentParts.label: The Monk's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse collection of tragedies; the Gg text occupies ff. 352r–362v.

contentParts.label: The Nun's Priest's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse beast fable; the Gg text occupies ff. 362v–371v, while Furnivall appends an end-link from Royal MS 17 D XV.

contentParts.label: Group G, Fragment VIII
contentParts.summary: Second Nun and Canon's Yeoman sequence.

contentParts.label: The Second Nun's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse saint's life; the Gg text occupies ff. 375r–380v.

contentParts.label: The Canon's Yeoman's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse alchemical satire with prologue; the Gg text occupies ff. 381r–393v.

contentParts.label: Group H, Fragment IX
contentParts.summary: Manciple sequence.

contentParts.label: The Manciple's Tale
contentParts.summary: Verse moral fable with prologue; the Gg text occupies ff. 393v–398v.

contentParts.label: Group I, Fragment X
contentParts.summary: Parson sequence and Chaucer's Retraction; Furnivall supplies the concluding leave-taking/Retraction passage from Harley MS 1758.

contentParts.label: The Parson's Tale
contentParts.summary: Prose penitential treatise with prologue; the Gg text occupies ff. 398v–443v, and Furnivall retains Tyrwhitt's paragraph divisions.

contentParts.label: Retraction
contentParts.summary: Prose authorial leave-taking at the end of the Parson sequence; Gg ends on f. 443v, with the edition's final supplied passage identified as Harley 1758.

contentParts.label: The spurious Tale of Gamelyn
contentParts.summary: A complete verse appendix transcribed by Furnivall from Sloane MS 1685 and explicitly labeled spurious; it is not counted among Chaucer's twenty-four tales.

contentParts.label: Teller drawings and allegorical figures

locators.value: “Drawings of the 23 Tellers” and “Drawings of 6 Tellers ... and 6 Allegorical Figures”

contentParts.summary: Printed woodcuts by W. H. Hooper: twenty-three teller portraits copied from the Ellesmere manuscript, followed by six teller and six Vice/Virtue figures copied from Gg.4.27. The XML's omitted back-matter division retains page milestones but not a textual encoding of this visual suite.

extent.description: The intellectual extent is the conventional twenty-four Canterbury tales in ten macro-fragments: twenty-two verse tales and two prose tales (Melibee and the Parson's Tale), plus non-tale prologues, links, epilogues, and the prose Retraction. The sections field therefore counts the ten internally linked fragment groups, not every editorial subdivision. Gamelyn and visual/textual appendices are apparatus and are assigned item_count 0. The repository XML contains 10 main BODY DIV1 groups, 51 DIV2 elements, 69 DIV3 elements, 18,286 L elements, 609 LG elements, 233 P elements, 1,209 MILESTONE elements, and 738 uniquely sequenced PB elements. The verse_groups and verse_lines fields remain null because raw LG/L totals include prose serialized as lines, rubrics, supplied passages, and editorial structure and have not been semantically reduced to defensible verse metrics.

formStatements.description: Edition-level form is mixed: predominantly verse, with twenty-two conventional verse tales, the prose Tale of Melibee and Parson's Tale, a substantial prose Retraction, prose editorial apparatus, and visual appendices.
The two prose tales and the prose Retraction.

summaries.value: Furnivall's one-manuscript Chaucer Society edition presents the unfinished Canterbury Tales chiefly from Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27, filling documented losses and links from seven identified manuscripts plus one unresolved witness labeled Z, E, or I. Its ten groups contain the conventional twenty-four tales—twenty-two verse and two prose—within a pilgrimage frame, followed by the prose Retraction. The printed volume also appends the non-Chaucerian Tale of Gamelyn, textual links, twenty-three teller portraits copied from the Ellesmere manuscript, and six teller plus six allegorical figures copied from Gg.4.27.

Furnivall orders the ten internally linked groups as A/I, B-alpha/II, D/V, E/VI, F/VII, C/IV, B-beta/III, G/VIII, H/IX, and I/X. This is the edition's sequence, not a claim that Chaucer fixed a final order.

The XML serializes the title page, ten main groups, Gamelyn appendix, and page milestones across 738 source images, but deliberately labels broad front and back sections omitted. The complete printed scan shows additional textual and visual appendices. Representation counts and the 738 PB sequence therefore describe the encoding, not the number of intellectual sections or a complete transcription of every printed surface.
